title: What Pediatric Endocrinologists Need to Know About Dyslipidemia

items.title: Why dyslipidemia screening in childhood matters
items.text: CVD is the leading U.S. cause of death; atherosclerosis begins in youth. Universal screening and early treatment—especially for familial hypercholesterolemia—can reduce future events.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Universal screening and early treatment have the potential to significantly reduce future CVD-related events in youth with familial hypercholesterolemia."

items.title: How U.S. guidelines define who to screen for dyslipidemia
items.text: Selective screening from age ≥2 with family risk; universal lipid screening for all children ages 9–11, repeated between 17–21 if initial result normal.
items.reference: Who to screen: "Universal screening of all children 9 to 11 years of age regardless of general health or the presence/absence of CVD risk factors."

items.title: Preferred cholesterol screening method in pediatrics
items.text: Fasting or nonfasting lipid panels are acceptable; nonfasting is often more practical. Direct LDL assay needed when triglycerides >400 mg/dL preclude Friedewald calculation.
items.reference: Question 2: What is the preferred method of cholesterol screening?: "Screening can be performed using a fasting or nonfasting lipid profile. A nonfasting blood sample is often more practical and efficient."

items.title: Familial hypercholesterolemia clinical pattern
items.text: In absence of secondary causes, marked LDL elevation (~1:200–1:500 prevalence) with premature family CVD; youth are often asymptomatic yet highest CVD risk.
items.reference: Question 2: Based on this patient's findings, which of the following is the most likely cause of her lipid abnormality?: "In the absence of secondary causes of hypercholesterolemia, the most likely diagnosis is familial hypercholesterolemia (~1:200-1:500)."

items.title: Why LDL thresholds trigger statin therapy in pediatric FH
items.text: FDA-approved for age ≥10 when LDL >190 mg/dL or >160 mg/dL with ≥1 additional risk factor; goal LDL ≤100 mg/dL or ≥50% reduction from baseline.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "All statins have been approved by the US FDA for use in children with familial hypercholesterolemia and should be recommended to patients aged 10 years and older when the LDL-cholesterol concentration is greater than 190 mg/dL (>4.92 mmol/L) or greater than 160 mg/dL (>4.14 mmol/L) in patients with 1 or more additional risk factors."

items.title: How long-term pravastatin data support childhood FH treatment
items.text: Clinical trials show statins are safe and improve carotid intima-media thickness; 20-year pravastatin follow-up showed remarkable absence of MACE vs affected parent.
items.reference: Question 3: Is there evidence that lipid-lowering medications, such as statins, are safe when prescribed at a young age? If so, are they effective?: "demonstrated a remarkable absence of major adverse cardiovascular events at age 40 years and older in contrast to the affected parent."

items.title: How to approach genetic testing in suspected FH
items.text: Test LDLR, APOB, PCSK9 with appropriate sequencing/deletion analysis; cascade testing of relatives when pathogenic variant found. Negative testing does not exclude FH or need for therapy.
items.reference: Question 4: Is there a role for genetic testing?: "Genetic testing is recommended for all individuals clinically suspected to have familial hypercholesterolemia."

items.title: Why cascade screening of relatives is recommended
items.text: Systematic first-degree relative cholesterol or genotype testing enhances detection; adults in the family often have greater short-term risk than affected children.
items.reference: Question 5: Now that you have initiated treatment, is there a role for cascade screening?: "Screening the family of a patient with familial hypercholesterolemia by systematically identifying first-degree relatives (ie, cascade screening), is recommended"

items.title: How pregnancy affects statin therapy in FH
items.text: Statins, ezetimibe, and niacin are category X—stop at least 4 weeks before conception; discontinue immediately if unintended pregnancy; withhold lipid drugs during breastfeeding.
items.reference: Case 1 (Continued): "These treatments SHOULD NOT BE USED during pregnancy. In the case of an unintended pregnancy, a woman with familial hypercholesterolemia should discontinue statins, ezetimibe, and niacin immediately."

items.title: Role of lipoprotein(a) in selected pediatric cases
items.text: Adult Lp(a) levels are set by age 2–5 years; >50 mg/dL (>100 nmol/L) affects ~1 in 5 individuals and is an independent causative CVD risk factor counted within LDL fraction.
items.reference: Advanced Lipid Testing: "Elevated lipoprotein (a), defined as greater than 50 mg/dL (>100 nmol/L), is the most common genetic dyslipidemia, affecting nearly 1 in 5 individuals in the United States."

items.title: Treatment goals differ by triglyceride severity
items.text: TG <150 mg/dL for health maintenance/CVD prevention; TG ≥500–999 mg/dL focus on pancreatitis prevention with fibrates/omega-3; extreme elevations need strict fat restriction.
items.reference: Table 4. Treatment of Hypertriglyceridemia: "Goal ... CVD Risk Prevention ... Avoid Pancreatitis"

items.title: Secondary causes to exclude before diagnosing FH
items.text: Screen hypothyroidism, liver/kidney disease, diabetes/obesity, and medications (steroids, isotretinoin, OCPs) when evaluating pediatric dyslipidemia.
items.reference: Table 3. Secondary Causes of Dyslipidemia: "Hypothyroidism ... Liver diseases ... Kidney diseases ... Diabetes mellitus ... Obesity, insulin resistance ... Medications"

items.question: A healthy 10-year-old girl had cholesterol screening at a well-child visit showing elevated LDL. Was this appropriate?
items.explanation: AAP/AHA recommend universal lipid screening at 9–11 years regardless of risk factors.
items.reference: Who to screen: "Universal screening of all children 9 to 11 years of age regardless of general health or the presence/absence of CVD risk factors."

items.question: A 16-year-old girl with LDL 172 mg/dL, BMI >95th percentile, acanthosis, HbA1c 6.2%, maternal statin use, and grandfather MI at 44. Most likely lipid diagnosis?
items.explanation: After excluding secondary causes, FH is most likely (~1:200–1:500) despite obesity-related metabolic findings.
items.reference: Question 2: Based on this patient's findings, which of the following is the most likely cause of her lipid abnormality?: "the most likely diagnosis is familial hypercholesterolemia (~1:200-1:500)."

items.question: A 16-year-old with FH has LDL 172 mg/dL and multiple risk factors (obesity, prediabetes, family history). Appropriate therapy?
options: Start statin after LDL >190 mg/dL only
Start statin (e.g., atorvastatin) with LDL goal ≤100 mg/dL
items.explanation: LDL >160 with risk factors meets FDA-approved pediatric statin indication; goal ≤100 or 50% reduction.
items.reference: Question 3: What treatment would you recommend? What are your treatment goals?: "greater than 160 mg/dL (>4.14 mmol/L) with 1 or more additional risk factor."

items.question: When starting atorvastatin in a sexually active 16-year-old girl with FH, what else is essential?
options: Recommend effective contraception and counsel on pregnancy statin risks
items.explanation: Statins are contraindicated in pregnancy; contraception counseling is mandatory in sexually active females.
items.reference: Case 1 (Continued): "Because she is sexually active, an oral contraceptive pill is also recommended."

items.question: A 17-year-old on atorvastatin for FH is found 8 weeks pregnant. Best management?
options: Continue atorvastatin for fetal LDL benefit
Stop statin, ezetimibe, and niacin immediately; counsel on pregnancy FH risks
Double statin dose for placental transfer blockade
Add ezetimibe for enhanced LDL lowering
items.explanation: Category X lipid drugs must stop immediately in pregnancy; prepregnancy counseling advised for all women on therapy.
items.reference: Case 1 (Continued): "a woman with familial hypercholesterolemia should discontinue statins, ezetimibe, and niacin immediately."

items.question: A patient with FH has triglycerides 165 mg/dL and BMI >95th percentile with HbA1c 6.2%. This suggests:
options: FH cannot coexist with elevated triglycerides
Possible insulin resistance/prediabetes as secondary contributor
Mandatory fibrate therapy for pancreatitis prevention
items.explanation: Elevated TG does not exclude FH; insulin resistance may contribute additional CVD risk.
items.reference: Question 2: Is this patient's elevated triglycerides of concern?: "the presence of an elevated value does not exclude familial hypercholesterolemia."

items.question: Preferred practical approach to pediatric lipid screening in clinic is often:
options: POC total cholesterol alone without HDL
items.explanation: Nonfasting samples are practical and acceptable per guidelines.
items.reference: Question 2: What is the preferred method of cholesterol screening?: "A nonfasting blood sample is often more practical and efficient."

items.question: When triglycerides exceed 400 mg/dL, LDL cholesterol should be:
items.explanation: High TG precludes reliable Friedewald LDL calculation.
items.reference: Question 2: What is the preferred method of cholesterol screening?: "Significant triglyceride elevation (eg, >400 mg/dL [>4.52 mmol/L]) precludes calculation of LDL cholesterol using the Friedewald formula, in which case LDL cholesterol can be measured with a direct assay."

items.question: Genetic testing for suspected FH should include:
items.explanation: Standard FH gene panel includes LDLR, APOB, PCSK9 with appropriate structural variant analysis.
items.reference: Question 4: Is there a role for genetic testing?: "genetic testing for suspected familial hypercholesterolemia should include analysis of LDLR, APOB, and PCSK9"

items.question: After identifying FH in an index child, recommended family approach is:
options: MRI coronary angiography for all siblings under 5
Statin for all relatives regardless of LDL
items.explanation: Cascade cholesterol or genotype testing of relatives is recommended.
items.reference: Question 5: Now that you have initiated treatment, is there a role for cascade screening?: "cascade screening), is recommended to enhance detection of individuals at-risk for familial hypercholesterolemia."

items.question: Data suggest women and children with FH are often:
options: Diagnosed earlier than men on average
items.explanation: European registry data show women less likely on lipid-lowering therapy and goal LDL.
items.reference: Question 1: Should treatment be delayed because the patient is female?: "Recent data from the European Atherosclerosis Society 2020 Virtual Congress suggest that female patients and children are generally undertreated."

items.question: Per Table 1, LDL ≥130 mg/dL in children is classified as:
items.explanation: Pediatric norms use population percentiles; ≥130 mg/dL is high.
items.reference: Table 1. Acceptable, Borderline, and High Plasma Lipid and Lipoprotein Concentrations for Children and Adolescents: "LDL cholesterol ... ≥130 mg/dL (SI): ≥3.37 mmol/L)"

items.question: A child with clinical FH has negative LDLR/APOB/PCSK9 testing. Next step?
options: Consider other genetic causes and continue therapy based on clinical risk
items.explanation: Negative testing does not exclude FH or obviate treatment; other genes and polygenic inheritance exist.
items.reference: Question 4: Is there a role for genetic testing?: "For individuals with negative genetic test results, other pathogenic variants ... and as-yet unidentified genes or polygenic inheritance should be considered."

items.statement: All U.S. children ages 9–11 should undergo universal cholesterol screening.
items.explanation: Current AAP/AHA recommendation.
items.reference: Who to screen: "Universal screening of all children 9 to 11 years of age regardless of general health or the presence/absence of CVD risk factors."

items.statement: If initial childhood cholesterol screening is normal, repeat testing is recommended between ages 17 and 21.
items.explanation: Stated in screening guidelines.
items.reference: Who to screen: "If the initial cholesterol value is normal, screening should be repeated between 17 and 21 years of age."

items.statement: Statins may be continued throughout pregnancy in women with familial hypercholesterolemia.
items.explanation: Category X—must discontinue in pregnancy and breastfeeding.
items.reference: Case 1 (Continued): "These treatments SHOULD NOT BE USED during pregnancy."

items.statement: Familial hypercholesterolemia affects approximately 1 in 200–500 individuals.
items.explanation: Cited prevalence range.
items.reference: Question 2: Based on this patient's findings, which of the following is the most likely cause of her lipid abnormality?: "familial hypercholesterolemia (~1:200-1:500)."

items.statement: Routine advanced lipoprotein subclass analysis is recommended for all children.
items.explanation: Not routinely recommended; may help selected cases.
items.reference: Advanced Lipid Testing: "routine measurement of lipoprotein (a), apolipoprotein B, apolipoprotein A1, and lipoprotein subclasses and their sizes by advanced lipoprotein analysis is not currently recommended"

items.statement: All statins are FDA-approved for use in children with familial hypercholesterolemia meeting LDL thresholds.
items.explanation: Main conclusions statement.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "All statins have been approved by the US FDA for use in children with familial hypercholesterolemia"

items.statement: A heart-healthy lifestyle alone is unlikely to prevent atherosclerosis in familial hypercholesterolemia.
items.explanation: Listed as barrier—lifestyle alone insufficient for FH.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "A heart-healthy lifestyle alone is unlikely to prevent atherosclerosis in individuals with familial hypercholesterolemia."

items.statement: Lipoprotein(a) levels are generally stable throughout an individual's lifespan after early childhood.
items.explanation: Adult levels achieved by 2–5 years and remain constant.
items.reference: Advanced Lipid Testing: "in general the level is stable and remains constant throughout the lifespan of an individual."

items.assertion: Assertion: Universal lipid screening at ages 9–11 can identify asymptomatic familial hypercholesterolemia.
items.reason: Reason: Cascade screening alone is more effective than universal testing.
items.explanation: Assertion true; cascade depends on index case—reason misstates comparative strategy.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "Universal testing has the advantage of identifying familial hypercholesterolemia in asymptomatic youth and young adults"

items.assertion: Assertion: Pediatric statin therapy improves noninvasive measures of atherosclerosis.
items.reason: Reason: No pediatric statin trial has ever measured carotid intima-media thickness.
items.explanation: Assertion true per trials; reason false.
items.reference: Question 3: Is there evidence that lipid-lowering medications, such as statins, are safe when prescribed at a young age? If so, are they effective?: "pharmacologic treatment is safe and effective and improves noninvasive measures of atherosclerosis, such as carotid intima-media thickness."

items.assertion: Assertion: Nonfasting lipid screening is acceptable in children.
items.reason: Reason: Food intake always invalidates LDL measurement in pediatrics.
items.explanation: Assertion true; direct LDL and Lp(a) minimally affected—reason false.
items.reference: Question 2: What is the preferred method of cholesterol screening?: "Screening can be performed using a fasting or nonfasting lipid profile."

items.assertion: Assertion: Elevated triglycerides do not exclude familial hypercholesterolemia.
items.reason: Reason: FH always presents with normal triglycerides.
items.explanation: Assertion true; most FH have normal TG but obesity can coexist—reason false.
items.reference: Question 2: Is this patient's elevated triglycerides of concern?: "the presence of an elevated value does not exclude familial hypercholesterolemia."

items.assertion: Assertion: Genetic testing is recommended for clinically suspected FH.
items.reason: Reason: A negative genetic test always means statins are unnecessary.
items.explanation: Assertion true; negative test does not exclude need for therapy—reason false.
items.reference: Question 4: Is there a role for genetic testing?: "some parents might assume that a lipid-lowering medication is unnecessary for children in whom no common genetic pathogenic variant is found."

items.assertion: Assertion: Statins should be stopped immediately if FH patient has unintended pregnancy.
items.reason: Reason: Statins are safe category A drugs in pregnancy.
items.explanation: Assertion true; category X—reason false.
items.reference: Case 1 (Continued): "instructions to stop statins, ezetimibe, and niacin at least 4 weeks before discontinuing contraception. These treatments SHOULD NOT BE USED during pregnancy."

items.assertion: Assertion: LDL goal in pediatric FH is ≤100 mg/dL or ≥50% reduction from baseline.
items.reason: Reason: Pediatric LDL goals are always <70 mg/dL regardless of age.
items.explanation: Assertion states guideline goal; <70 not specified here—reason false.
items.reference: Question 3: What treatment would you recommend? What are your treatment goals?: "The treatment goal is an LDL-cholesterol concentration of 100 mg/dL or less (≤2.59 mmol/L), or a minimum of 50% reduction from baseline."

items.assertion: Assertion: Cascade screening of first-degree relatives is recommended after FH diagnosis.
items.reason: Reason: Only children, never adults, need family screening.
items.explanation: Assertion true; adults often at greater short-term risk—reason false.
items.reference: Question 5: Now that you have initiated treatment, is there a role for cascade screening?: "adults in the family, many of whom may be unaware of their hypercholesterolemia, are at greater short-term risk than affected children."
